revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== 脚注 ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}

The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}

[[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Fujisato, Akita]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq-1月4日 (旧暦)-qq]] [[ja:素波里神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}}

{{神社 |名称 = 素波里神社 |画像 = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] |所在地 = [[秋田県]][[山本郡]][[藤里町]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40|緯度分 = 19|緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140|経度分 = 13|経度秒 = 12.61 |祭神 = [[日本武尊]]、[[素盞嗚神]]
もと[[不動明王]]、[[牛頭天王]] |社格 = 郷社 |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月28日]] |神事 = |地図 = Japan Akita }} '''素波里神社'''（すばりじんじゃ）は、[[秋田県]][[山本郡]][[藤里町]]粕毛字岩合にある神社である。ご神体は不動明王立像である。その他に脇侍、[[矜羯羅童子]]、[[制多迦童子]]の木像がある。「藤里町」の名前の2字目にある「里」は、「素波里」の「里」から取っている。 == 沿革 == 素波里神社の創建は不明であるが、[[1712年]]（[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]元年）の神社調べに記載されていることから、それ以前に創建されたことは確実である。[[1843年]]（[[天保]]14年）の『粕毛村神社書上書』によれば、開基は奥州錦戸殿とある。錦戸殿は[[1200年]]（[[正治]]2年）4月に死亡していることから、[[建久]]年間（[[1190年]] - 1200年前後）の開基ではないかとも言われている。 古名を'''岩切沢不動堂'''、山号は'''青黒山'''、寺号は'''峰大寺'''といったが、[[神仏分離]]によって社名を素波里神社に、祭神を[[不動明王]]から日本武尊に改め、末社の[[牛頭天王]]を素盞嗚神に改めた。 [[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|素波里不動の滝]] == 社殿 == 創建当時の社は、現在の社殿がある西側台地の滝を望める場所にあったとされる。[[1702年]]（[[元禄]]15年）に郷中で創建、[[1813年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]10年）藤琴村の市川孫兵衛が新築して寄進、[[1846年]]（[[弘化]]3年）と[[1938年]]（[[昭和]]13年）にそれぞれ再建されて現在に至る。[[1972年]]（昭和47年）の大洪水の際、[[素波里ダム]]の緊急放水により浸水被害を受ける。その後、防水のために堤防が作られ、以前のような川の畔に立つ社の景観を失った。 奉納物は[[1692年]]（元禄5年）の[[鰐口]]を始め多数ある。[[1867年]]（[[慶応]]3年）と[[1869年]]（[[明治]]2年）の俳句献額には、幕末期や明治初期の秋田県俳句界著名人の献額がある。また、社の前には[[灯籠|石灯籠]]や[[狛犬]]などが献納されている。[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）には、地元出身のバスケットボール指導者の[[加藤廣志]]より「能代工業バスケットボール全国優勝30回記念」の碑と水盤の石が献納されている。加藤廣志は2018年[[平成]]30年3月に亡くなったが、妻テイが同年7月に同神社の手水舎の屋根と柱を寄進している。手水舎は幅4メートル、高さ3メートル。銅板葺きで、バスケットゴールの柱に見立てたイチイの支柱が特徴である。加藤廣志は、能代工業バスケットボール部の監督を退いてからも年に一度は参拝し、亡くなる前年も訪れていたが、水盤の石が長く野ざらしに近い状態だったのを気にかけていたという[http://www.hokuu.co.jp/2018.kiji7/26-31.html#nijuuku 北羽新報][秋田魁新報 2018年7月29日　地域（20）]。 神社奥すぐに[[素波里ダム]]の施設があるが、この脇を通り過ぎて左奥に行くと、素波里不動の滝がある。滝下には数個の献納された観音像がある。 == 伝説 == 口伝によれば、[[円仁]]が自作の不動明王を祀ったのが始まりだとか、安倍氏が敗れてこの地に逃れて来たときに、背負ってきた不動像をこの地に置いたのが始まりだとか、粕毛村谷地にあった館に立ち寄った[[比内浅利氏]]の氏神が始まりだという説もある。いずれの説も、戦いに敗れて素波里の奥地にある大開（現在の猿ヶ瀬素波里園地）を目指したものの、道が険しく素波里に置き去りにしたものを、地元の里人が小屋を建てて安置したものであることは一致している。 == 菅江真澄の記録 == [[菅江真澄]]は[[1802年]]に素波里神社を訪れ、その内容を『しげき山本』に記している。真澄が神社につくと「青黒山」と書いた額があった。鉄製の小さな剣が神社の梁に隙間無く並べられていた。不動の滝に行くと滝の奥にも道があって3人の男が歩いていった。神社に戻り、川をさかのぼって来た舟に乗せてもらい「すばり」という迫りたつ岩の間を進んだ。岩は高くそびえ、淵は青く深かったが、水は底まで澄んで透き通り清らかだった。なお進むと、岩壁がますます迫ってきて、桃源郷を訪れたような気分になったと記している。その後、雷鳴が鳴り響いて来たので舟は引き返している『しげき山本』菅江真澄。 == 石井忠行の記録 == [[久保田藩|久保田]][[藩士]]であった[[石井忠行]]は、『伊頭園茶話』第15巻（明治7年）に素波里神社（不動）のことを記録している『新秋田叢書11巻』収録。石井は久保田藩の巡検使だった井口経包(亘)の『六郡御界目巡回雑記』（[[1789年]]）を抜粋しているが、井口は実際に素波里神社を訪れておらず、[[粕毛村]]の肝煎だった安保万右衛門からの聞き取りを記しているため、石井は井口の記録を修正する形で素波里神社を紹介している。粕毛川を2里ほど遡れば素波里の不動があり、西向かいの滝があることを記している。また、不動堂の前の川から川上に舟で行くと、石に切れ目があり、塩俵を積み重ねたように見える所があり、他に見たことがないような面白い景色を見ることができるとしている。また、不動の滝は六尺にならない程度に細く流れているとしている。石井忠行が見た場所には、現在素波里ダムが建設されている。 == 歌人の訪問 == [[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|素波里 石井露月の歌碑]] [[1928年]]（昭和3年）8月22日[[名和三幹竹]]、[[石井露月]]、[[島田五空]]など10名ほどの歌人が素波里を訪問した。素波里神社や周囲の風景の中で沢山の句を詠みながら散策した。その後、穂波（安保小市郎）校長に歓待され[[米田小学校]]に宿泊する。翌23日、帰路[[粕毛村]]村長の佐々木隆吉宅を訪問し、そこで[[佐々木北涯]]の素波里行の遺筆を見て、正午に[[きみまち阪]]を散策した名和三幹竹『[{{NDLDC|1470474/17}} 能代から素波里へ]』、『懸葵 25』収録、1928年島田五空『五空遺文 有用無用』、五空先生頌徳碑建立会、1933年、p.176-180。石井露月の歌は鮎を歌ったものが多い石井露月『[{{NDLDC|1225616/253}} 蜩を聴きつゝ : 露月文集]』、昭和10年。石井露月は27日、戸米川小学校での校長の送別会でのあいさつの壇上で倒れそのまま死亡する。生家近くの秋田市雄和女米木猫沢の高尾神社里宮の境内には露月の歌碑が建っている。その内容は「花野ゆく耳にきのふの峡の声」とあり、この歌碑は素波里峡谷を歌ったものとされている。秋田県内の露月の歌碑は11基ある鎌田宏『あきた句碑物語』、無明舎出版 、1988年、p.112。 素波里には5体の石仏の前に[[1991年]]（平成3年）12月建立された石井露月の歌碑がある。「君を訪へば鮎の瀬音の高まさる」 == その他 == * 素波里ダムができる前はこの地は左右の崖が迫る景勝地で、[[菅江真澄]]、[[石井忠行]]、[[狩野旭峰]]、[[石井露月]]その他の有名な文人墨客が多く訪れ、探訪記を残している。 * 昔日の祭典は近郊からの参拝者が非常に多く、大変にぎやかであったといわれる。 * 「すばり」という語は「すぼまって狭い」という意味で、同じ藤里町でも藤琴川の奥に{{ウィキ座標|40|22|20.45|N|140|19|34.8|E||素波里}}という地名があり、同じく渓谷地形である。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 藤里町史編纂委員会『藤里町史』藤里町、2013年 {{神道 横}} {{Pref-stub|pref=秋田県}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すはりしんしや}} [[Category:秋田県の神社]] [[Category:秋田県の旧郷社]] [[Category:祇園神社]] [[Category:藤里町の建築物]] [[Category:藤里町の歴史]]

'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]

The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**

In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**

[[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Fujisato, Akita]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[ja:素波里神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|日本武尊}}" to "|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**.== See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712年） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14年) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2年), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3年) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13年). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972年}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802年}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712nd year }} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year }} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15年), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813rd year }} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10年) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972nd year }} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802nd year }} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}

[[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Fujisato, Akita]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:素波里神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712nd year }} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200th year }} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year }} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813rd year }} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938th year }} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972nd year }} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802nd year }} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==

[[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Gōsha shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Fujisato, Akita]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Subari Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Subari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:素波里神社]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Subari Shrine}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712nd year }} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200th year }} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year }} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813rd year }} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938th year }} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972nd year }} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802nd year }} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町|12=draft}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|12=draft}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日|12=draft}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|12=draft}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|12=draft}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|12=draft}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712nd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳|12=draft}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|12=draft}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|12=draft}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治|12=draft}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久|12=draft}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|12=draft}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|12=draft}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=draft}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702nd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|12=draft}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813rd year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化|12=draft}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年|12=draft}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化|12=draft}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938th year|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|ja|1972nd year|12=draft}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム|12=draft}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム|12=draft}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|12=draft}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft}} visited in {{ill|1802|ja|1802nd year|12=draft}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|ja|久保田藩|12=draft}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|12=draft}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=draft}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=draft}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=draft}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Pref-stub|Akita Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=draft|12=draft}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=draft|12=draft}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=draft|12=draft}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|ja|素波里ダム|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
