{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamoto District, Akita|qq=}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisato, Akita|qq=}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|1=Acala|lt=Fudō Myōō|2=ja|3=不動明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|qq=}}, {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|qq=}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|ja|7月28日|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 28|qq=}} | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto|qq=}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|ja|藤里町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujisato, Akita|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|ja|山本郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamamoto District, Akita|qq=}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|ja|秋田県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akita Prefecture|qq=}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|1=Acala|lt=Fudō Myōō|2=ja|3=不動明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|qq=}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|ja|1712年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1712|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōtoku (era)|ja|正徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōtoku (era)|qq=}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|ja|天保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpō|qq=}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|ja|1200年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1200|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|ja|正治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōji (era)|qq=}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū (era)|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkyū (era)|qq=}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|ja|不動明王|1=Acala|lt=Fudō Myōō|2=ja|3=不動明王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|13=User:Immanuelle/Acala|qq=}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|13=User:Immanuelle/Gozu Tennō|qq=}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]

It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|ja|1702年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1702|qq=}} ({{ill|Genroku|ja|元禄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku|qq=}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|ja|1813年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1813|qq=}} ({{ill|Bunka (Japanese era)|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka (Japanese era)|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|qq=}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|ja|1846年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1846|qq=}} ({{ill|Kōka|ja|弘化|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka|qq=}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|ja|1938年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1938|qq=}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 13rd year ). '''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Pref-stub|Akita Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). [[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]

The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.

According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== 参考文献 ==" to "== Bibliography =="
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==

revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.

* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24871133]] Wikidata name: Subari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = [[File:Subari Zinzya.jpg|250px]] | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}, [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Pref-stub|Akita Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|素波里神社|version=104143241|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Fujisato, Akita]] [[Category:History of Akita Prefecture]]
[[Category:Completion tier 1]]
}}

== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

[[sn:Subari Shrine]] [[ja:素波里神社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Fujisato Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fujisato]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24871133]] Wikidata name: Subari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|qid=Q1206039}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24871133]] Wikidata name: Subari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|qid=Q1206039}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.

[[ja:素波里神社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Fujisato Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fujisato]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q24871133}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24871133]] Wikidata name: Subari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|WD=Q1206039}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|qq=|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|qq=|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|qq=|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|qq=|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|qq=|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|qq=|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]]

[[ja:素波里神社]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Akita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Fujisato Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Fujisato]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q24871133}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q24871133]] Wikidata name: Subari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]][[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]][[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]]粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = [[:en:July 28|July 28]] | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|WD=Q1206039}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Fujisato, Akita|Fujisato, Akita]], [[:en:Yamamoto District, Akita|Yamamoto District, Akita]], [[:en:Akita Prefecture|Akita Prefecture]]. The main deity enshrined is [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the [[:en:1712|1712]] ([[:en:正德|Shōtoku (era)]]元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ([[:en:Tenpō|Tenpō]] 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in [[:en:1200|1200]] ([[:en:Shōji (era)|Shōji (era)]] 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the [[:en:Kenkyū|Kenkyū (era)]] period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in [[:en:1702|1702]] ([[:en:Genroku|Genroku]] 15th year ), rebuilt in [[:en:1813|1813]] ([[:en:Culture|Bunka]] 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in [[:en:1846|1846]] ([[:en:Kōka|Kōka]] 3rd year ) and [[:en:1938|1938]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa]] 13rd year ). During the [[:en:1972|1972]] (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]] caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of [[:en:Subari Dam|Subari Dam]], visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]] statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in [[:en:1802|1802]] and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The [[:en:Kubota Domain|Kubota Domain]] samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.

The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
