[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:素波里神社]]
{{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|en|July 28|WD=Q2718|lt=July 28}} | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|WD=Q1206039}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|en|1712|WD=Q6871|lt=1712}} ({{ill|正德|en|正德|WD=Q11123425|lt=Shōtoku (era)}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|en|1200|WD=Q5224|lt=1200}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|en|Shōji (era)|WD=Q1199890|lt=Shōji (era)}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū (era)}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|en|1813|WD=Q6937|lt=1813}} ({{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|en|1846|WD=Q7640|lt=1846}} ({{ill|Kōka|en|Kōka|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|en|1938|WD=Q18645|lt=1938}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in {{ill|1802|en|1802|WD=Q6883|lt=1802}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|WD=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Pref-stub|Akita Prefecture}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita|1=Yamamoto District, Akita}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita|1=Fujisato, Akita}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|en|July 28|WD=Q2718|lt=July 28|1=July 28}} | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|WD=Q1206039|1=Fujisato, Akita}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita|1=Fujisato, Akita}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita|1=Yamamoto District, Akita}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|en|1712|WD=Q6871|lt=1712|1=1712}} ({{ill|正德|en|正德|WD=Q11123425|lt=Shōtoku (era)|1=正德}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|en|1200|WD=Q5224|lt=1200|1=1200}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|en|Shōji (era)|WD=Q1199890|lt=Shōji (era)|1=Shōji (era)}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū (era)|1=Kenkyū}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|en|1813|WD=Q6937|lt=1813|1=1813}} ({{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|en|1846|WD=Q7640|lt=1846|1=1846}} ({{ill|Kōka|en|Kōka|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka|1=Kōka}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|en|1938|WD=Q18645|lt=1938|1=1938}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972|1=1972}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in {{ill|1802|en|1802|WD=Q6883|lt=1802|1=1802}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|WD=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Subari Shrine|native_name = 素波里神社 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|image = File:Subari Zinzya.jpg | address = {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}{{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita|1=Yamamoto District, Akita}}{{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita|1=Fujisato, Akita}}粕毛字岩合2 | 緯度度 = 40 | 緯度分 = 19 | 緯度秒 = 57.58 | 経度度 = 140 | 経度分 = 13 | 経度秒 = 12.61 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}} | 創建 = 不詳（記録上1712nd year ） | 旧社格 = 郷社 | 例祭日 = {{ill|July 28|en|July 28|WD=Q2718|lt=July 28|1=July 28}} | 地図 = Japan Akita | prefecture= {{ill|Fujisato|WD=Q1206039|1=Fujisato, Akita}} | coordinates= {{Coord|40.33266666666667|N|140.22016666666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Subari Shrine''' (素波里神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} shrine located in {{ill|Fujisato, Akita|en|Fujisato, Akita|WD=Q1206039|lt=Fujisato, Akita|1=Fujisato, Akita}}, {{ill|Yamamoto District, Akita|en|Yamamoto District, Akita|WD=Q1155896|lt=Yamamoto District, Akita|1=Yamamoto District, Akita}}, {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|WD=Q81863|lt=Akita Prefecture|1=Akita Prefecture}}. The main deity enshrined is {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, with additional wooden statues of {{ill|Kongara-dōji|ja|矜羯羅童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kongara-dōji|1=Kiṃkara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11583862}} and {{ill|Seitaka-dōji|ja|制多迦童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seitaka-dōji|1=Ceṭaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11396963}}. The second kanji in "Fujisato" (藤里) originates from "Subari" (素波里).
== 沿革 ==
The exact founding date of Subari Shrine is unknown, but it is mentioned in the {{ill|1712|en|1712|WD=Q6871|lt=1712|1=1712}} ({{ill|正德|en|正德|WD=Q11123425|lt=Shōtoku (era)|1=正德}}元年) shrine records, confirming that it existed before then. According to the 1843 ({{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|WD=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}} 14th year ) "Kasuage Village Shrine Report" (粕毛村神社書上書), the shrine was established by the {{ill|Nishikido clan|ja|錦戸殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishikido clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of Oshu. Nishikido-dono passed away in {{ill|1200|en|1200|WD=Q5224|lt=1200|1=1200}} ({{ill|Shōji (era)|en|Shōji (era)|WD=Q1199890|lt=Shōji (era)|1=Shōji (era)}} 2nd year ), suggesting the shrine was founded around the {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū (era)|1=Kenkyū}} period (1190-1200).
The shrine was originally called '''Iwakirisawa Fudodō''' (岩切沢不動堂), with the mountain name '''Seikokuzan''' (青黒山) and the temple name '''Hodai-ji''' (峰大寺). However, during the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} period, it was renamed "Subari Shrine," and its main deity changed from {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} to {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, while the enshrined {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} was replaced by {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞嗚神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.
[[File:Subari Fudô no Taki.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Subari Fudo Falls]]
== 社殿 ==
The original shrine was located on the western plateau overlooking the waterfall. It was built by the local community in {{ill|1702|en|1702|WD=Q6819|lt=1702|1=1702}} ({{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku|1=Genroku}} 15th year ), rebuilt in {{ill|1813|en|1813|WD=Q6937|lt=1813|1=1813}} ({{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}} 10th year ) by Ichikawa Sonbei of Fujikoto Village, and reconstructed again in {{ill|1846|en|1846|WD=Q7640|lt=1846|1=1846}} ({{ill|Kōka|en|Kōka|WD=Q1325317|lt=Kōka|1=Kōka}} 3rd year ) and {{ill|1938|en|1938|WD=Q18645|lt=1938|1=1938}} ({{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 13rd year ). During the {{ill|1972|en|1972|WD=Q2476|lt=1972|1=1972}} (Shōwa 47) flood, emergency water discharge from {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}} caused severe water damage, leading to the construction of flood barriers, altering the shrine’s original riverside setting.
Offerings at the shrine include: - **A temple gong (鰐口) from 1692 (Genroku 5)** - **Haiku dedication plaques from 1867 (Keiō 3) and 1869 (Meiji 2)** - **A commemorative stone marking the Noshiro High School Basketball Team’s 30th national victory (1989) by coach Hiroshi Kato** - **A hand-washing station (2018), donated by Kato’s wife, Tei**
To the left of {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}}, visitors can find **Subari Fudō Falls**, with several consecrated Kannon statues below it.
== 伝説 ==
According to oral tradition: - **Monk Ennin** enshrined his handmade {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}} statue here. - **The Abe clan**, fleeing after their defeat, abandoned a Fudō statue here. - **The Asari clan of Hinai** used it as their tutelary shrine.
In all stories, defeated warriors carried a sacred statue into the remote Subari region but left it behind, and local villagers later built a shrine for it.
== 菅江真澄の記録 ==
{{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} visited in {{ill|1802|en|1802|WD=Q6883|lt=1802|1=1802}} and described his experience in *Shigeki Yamamoto* (しげき山本). He wrote about: - **A signboard reading "Seikokuzan" (青黒山)** - **Hundreds of iron miniature swords embedded in the shrine beams** - **A waterfall with a hidden path leading beyond it** - **A boat trip up the river through "Subari"—a narrow gorge with towering cliffs, deep blue water, and crystal-clear reflections** - **Feeling as if he had reached a paradise-like land**
== 石井忠行の記録 ==
The {{ill|Kubota Domain|en|Kubota Domain|WD=Q1067720|lt=Kubota Domain|1=Kubota Domain}} samurai {{ill|Ishii Tadayuki|ja|石井忠行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Tadayuki|1=Ishii Tadatsura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27920525}} described the shrine in *Izuen Sawa* (*伊頭園茶話*, 1874). He corrected earlier reports, stating: - **The Subari Fudō is located two ri upriver from Kasuke Village.** - **A boat ride upstream reveals "stacked salt-bale-like" rocks.** - **The Fudō Falls are about six shaku (1.8m) wide.**
The **modern Subari Dam now covers much of this landscape**.
== 歌人の訪問 ==
[[File:Subari Isii Rogetu no Kahi.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Ishii Rogetsu's haiku monument]]
In **1928**, notable poets including: - **{{ill|Nawa Sankanchiku|ja|名和三幹竹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nawa Sankanchiku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}** - **{{ill|Ishii Rogetsu|ja|石井露月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishii Rogetsu|1=Rogetsu Ishii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585142}}** - **{{ill|Shimada Gokū|ja|島田五空|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimada Gokū|1=Gokū Shimada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11476854}}**
visited the shrine and composed many verses. Rogetsu’s poems focused on **ayu fish and the river’s sounds**. One of his haiku, "Kimi o toe-ba ayu no seoto no takamaru," was inscribed on a monument at the site in **1991**.
== その他 ==
- Before **Subari Dam**, the area was a renowned scenic spot. - The shrine’s annual festival was once a major regional event. - "Subari" means **"a place where the valley narrows,"** found in multiple locations within **Fujisato Town**. == See Also ==
* {{ill|Subari Dam|en|Subari Dam|WD=Q19965851|lt=Subari Dam|1=Subari Dam}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Masumi|1=Sugae Masumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619285}} * {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book|和書|author=藤里町史編纂委員会|title=藤里町史|publisher=藤里町|year=2013}}
